title: Cast Care and Neurovascular Assessment
subtitle: A patient with a new extremity cast needs neurovascular monitoring and cast care. The nurse performs the checks (the P's), recognizes compartment syndrome early, and responds to this limb-threatening emergency.

patient.name: Cast & Neurovascular Care
patient.admitDate: A patient has a newly applied extremity cast. The nurse performs neurovascular checks, provides cast care, and recognizes compartment syndrome.

vitals.temp: Color, temp, pulse, refill, move, sense

nursesNote: A patient has a newly applied extremity cast. The nurse performs neurovascular checks distal to the cast — assessing the P's: Pain, Pallor (color/temperature), Pulselessness (pulses and capillary refill < 3 seconds), Paresthesia (sensation), and Paralysis (movement) — and compares the casted limb to the opposite side. In the first 24-48 hours the limb is elevated with ice to reduce swelling, and a plaster cast is left open to air and handled with the palms (no fingertip pressure that dents it). The nurse does not insert objects to scratch inside the cast, petals rough edges, and keeps it clean and dry. Severe pain unrelieved by opioids and worsened by passive stretch, tense swelling, and paresthesia signal compartment syndrome, a limb-threatening emergency — the nurse keeps the limb at heart level (not elevated), loosens constriction, and notifies the provider STAT for cast bivalving and possible fasciotomy. Pulselessness, pallor, and paralysis are late signs. Hot spots, foul odor, or drainage suggest infection or skin breakdown under the cast.
providerOrders: Perform neurovascular checks distal to the cast and compare both limbs
Elevate with ice in the first 24-48 hours to reduce swelling
Keep the cast clean and dry; do not insert objects inside it
Report pain unrelieved by opioids, paresthesia, or a tense, swollen limb
For suspected compartment syndrome, keep limb at heart level and notify STAT

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What is true about neurovascular and cast assessment?

opts.text: Check color, temperature, pulses, capillary refill, movement, and sensation distal to the cast
Capillary refill should be less than 3 seconds
Severe pain unrelieved by opioids and worse on passive stretch is a red flag
Numbness and tingling distal to the cast are normal and expected
Compare the casted extremity to the opposite side
A cool, pale, pulseless extremity is an expected finding after casting

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.sub: What guides compartment-syndrome and cast care?

opts.text: Compartment syndrome is a limb-threatening emergency
Pulselessness, pallor, and paralysis are LATE signs of compartment syndrome
Elevate a newly casted extremity to reduce swelling in the first 24-48 hours
A plaster cast should be covered and pressed to speed drying
Inserting an object to scratch inside the cast is acceptable for itching
Hot spots, foul odor, or drainage suggest infection or skin breakdown

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order a routine neurovascular check of a casted limb

opts.text: Assess pain level and ask about numbness or tingling
Inspect color and check skin temperature
Assess capillary refill and distal pulses
Check movement and sensation distal to the cast
Compare to the other extremity and document
Warm, pink toes with capillary refill under 3 seconds
Able to wiggle toes with intact sensation
Severe pain unrelieved by opioids and worse on passive stretch
Numbness, tingling, and inability to move the toes
Cool, pale, mottled foot with a weak or absent pulse
Mild discomfort relieved by elevation and prescribed analgesia
Foul odor and drainage staining the cast
Slight initial swelling that decreases with elevation

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: Suspected compartment syndrome: severe pain, tense swelling, paresthesia
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Stop elevating; keep the limb at heart level
Loosen any constrictive dressing without delay
Notify the provider STAT
Prepare for cast bivalving/removal and possible fasciotomy
Continue neurovascular checks and document
Elevating a newly casted limb to reduce swelling
Elevating the limb above the heart when compartment syndrome is suspected
Performing neurovascular checks and comparing both limbs
Inserting a coat hanger to scratch an itch inside the cast
Reporting pain that is unrelieved by opioids
Pressing on a wet plaster cast with fingertips
Keeping the cast clean and dry and petaling rough edges
Ignoring a hot spot and foul odor over the cast

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Reduction of Risk Potential, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
